summary: Bath County is just about seven miles from where I live, right at the Bath County line. It's really close, only about a mile away from my home in Montgomery County. Growing up, I always knew how close Bath County was, and it’s part of the local geography that shaped our community.

I was born and raised here in Montgomery County, and I've lived in Mount Sterling all my life. My family has been here since the late 1700s and early 1800s, so we have deep roots in this county. It’s a small town with wonderful memories, and it’s where we raised our kids and built our family history.

Mount Sterling is my hometown where I’ve lived all my life. My family started the McCormick Lumber Company here back in 1880, right down by Hinkston Creek and the railroad. It’s a place rich in history, from Native American mounds to being the center of Court Day, and it’s where our family business thrived for over a century.

My family started the McCormick Lumber Company in 1880 in Mount Sterling, near Hinkston Creek and the railroad. It was founded by my great-great-grandfather, John L. McCormick, and stayed in the family for six generations until we closed in 2011. The creek was essential for running the steam engines early on, but flooding eventually forced us to shut down after 131 years in business.

Hinkston Creek was vital to our lumber company because we needed the water to run the steam engines before electricity was common. It’s located in the low country of Mount Sterling and has a long history, including being near a large Indian mound associated with the Adena culture. Unfortunately, as the town grew and more asphalt was laid down, the creek started flooding regularly, which eventually caused us to close the lumber company.

John L. McCormick, my great-great-grandfather, founded the McCormick Lumber Company in 1880. The name John has been passed down through the family, with me being John G. McCormick III. He was the one who saw the opportunity to start the lumber business in Mount Sterling near the railroad and Hinkston Creek, setting the foundation for our family legacy.

The University of Kentucky did some archaeological research on the large Indian mound that used to stand at the corner of Queen and Locust in Mount Sterling. My uncle Ollie McCormick told me about their work, but sadly the mound was leveled for unknown reasons. It was connected to the Adena culture and would have been a great historical site to preserve.

The Adena culture is connected to a large Indian mound that once stood in Mount Sterling near our lumber company. It was about 40 feet tall and significant historically, but unfortunately, it was torn down for reasons no one really knows. The University of Kentucky researched it, and my family has always been aware of its importance in the area's prehistoric past.

Court Day has been a big part of my life since I was a kid, starting around 1964 when I was about ten years old. My family and I have been involved for over 58 years, renting spaces and participating in committees. It’s a huge event in Mount Sterling, drawing crowds of up to 30,000 people from places like Lexington and Frankfort, and I’ve earned the nickname 'Mister Court Day' from Mayor Al Botts because of my long-standing involvement.

Al Botts, the mayor, gave me the nickname 'Mister Court Day' because I’ve been involved in the event longer than anyone else, renting spaces for over 58 years. He started calling me that, and even though I didn’t like it at first, it stuck because of my deep connection to Court Day and my role as a landowner and committee member.

When I was a kid, my family lived out on Winchester Road in my dad’s big house, which was built in 1808. It was a great place to grow up, and during Court Day, we would park cars there to make some money. That spot was close enough to the lumber company and the railroad tracks where most of the Court Day activities happened.

Many of the people who came to Court Day were from Lexington, Kentucky, along with others from Frankfort and nearby areas. They would come every year, and some even became regular customers who knew us since we were kids parking cars. Lexington was one of the main cities contributing to the large crowds of about 30,000 people at Court Day.

Frankfort was another city where many Court Day visitors came from, along with Lexington and other parts of Kentucky. People from Frankfort would come back year after year, and we got to know a lot of them through parking cars and participating in the event. It was a big regional attraction drawing crowds from these nearby cities.
